draft.introduction: De Trinitate Book 15 is the culmination of Augustine’s fifteen-book exploration of the Trinity, where he finds in the human mind’s acts of memory, understanding, and will an image (imago) of the triune God—while acknowledging the analogy’s ultimate inadequacy.

translation.text: We have said as much as we can, not as much as we ought—for who can speak of you as you deserve? Yet I dare to say that I know these three: I remember, I understand, I will. We are an image, however faint, of the Trinity. Let us seek your face evermore, O Lord.
translation.original_text: Diximus quantum dicere potuimus, non quantum debuimus

translator_notes.note: Augustine’s psychological analogy for the Trinity—memory/understanding/will as image of Father/Son/Spirit—dominated Western Trinitarian theology until Barth and Rahner. Its brilliance lies in finding trinity in unity: one mind with three inseparable operations.
